skill.name: test-echo-tool
skill.description: A small Enact test skill that returns the provided text unchanged.
skill.summary: Echo input text to verify Enact tool execution and integration flow.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported one external command pattern and two weak cryptography patterns. The weak cryptography hits on SKILL.md lines 5 and 13 are false positives because they are plain description text, but the skill does execute an echo command with user-controlled text interpolation. No prompt injection, network access, filesystem access, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Shell Command
medium_findings.description: The skill defines an echo command that includes the text input placeholder. This appears intended for a simple test echo tool, but unsafe interpolation could allow shell metacharacters in the input to affect command execution depending on the Enact runtime escaping behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable command directly includes the user-controlled text placeholder. The risk depends on runtime escaping, so this is a real concern but not confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Match In Description
low_findings.description: Static analysis marked the skill description as a weak cryptography issue. The line only describes that the tool echoes input for testing, and no cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is metadata text, not executable code or a crypto API call. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic behavior at this location.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Match In Input Description
low_findings.description: Static analysis marked the input field description as a weak cryptography issue. The line only labels the text parameter and contains no cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a short human-readable schema description. No code, algorithm name, key handling, or cryptographic operation appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive External Command Match In Usage Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged the bash usage block as command execution evidence. The fenced example is documentation, not executable skill logic, although the skill does define a separate echo command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a Markdown usage example. They help users run the tool but are not executed by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command With Dynamic Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The command string contains a dynamic input placeholder inside a shell command. Publication should require confirmation that the runtime safely escapes user input before shell execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder is present in a command field and represents user-controlled data. The available file does not show the runtime escaping rules.

content.value_statement: Teams need a simple way to verify that Enact tool calls pass input and output correctly. This skill echoes supplied text so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows can confirm basic integration behavior.
actual_capabilities: Accepts a single text input field.
Returns the same text in an output field.
Uses an Alpine container image for execution.
Provides a minimal CLI usage example.
limitations: It does not transform, validate, or enrich the supplied text.
It is intended for testing rather than production automation.
It exposes only one required input field.
Shell escaping behavior depends on the Enact runtime.

use_cases.title: Verify Tool Wiring
use_cases.description: Confirm that an AI assistant can call an Enact skill and receive structured output.

use_cases.title: Test CLI Fixtures
use_cases.description: Use a predictable echo response when checking local Enact command behavior.

use_cases.title: Demonstrate Minimal Skills
use_cases.description: Show new contributors the smallest useful pattern for a text input and output skill.

prompt_templates.title: Echo A Short Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the echo tool with the text: Hello from my test.

prompt_templates.title: Check Exact Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the echo tool with this sentence and confirm the returned text matches exactly: Release check passed.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Integration Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Call the echo tool with a unique test phrase, then report whether the tool call and response parsing both succeeded.

prompt_templates.title: Probe Text Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the echo tool with punctuation, spaces, and numbers, then summarize whether the returned text preserved each character.

output_examples.output: The tool returns Hello as the output text.
The response contains the same phrase, with no intended transformation.

best_practices: Compare the returned text with the original input.
Review runtime escaping before testing shell metacharacters.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to process secrets or credentials.
Do not treat it as a production text processing tool.
Do not assume it validates unsafe shell input.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It returns the text value that the caller provides.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for developers testing Enact tool execution and AI assistant integrations.

faq.question: Does it change the input text?
faq.answer: No. The documented purpose is to echo the supplied text unchanged.

faq.question: Can it access the network?
faq.answer: No evidence of network access was found in the reviewed skill file.

faq.question: Is it safe for secrets?
faq.answer: No. Use non-sensitive test strings because echoed text may appear in logs or transcripts.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill interpolates user text into a shell command, so escaping behavior should be verified before publication.
